title: Longest Common Subsequence
description: 2D DP applied to two STRINGS instead of a grid or an item list: finding the longest subsequence common to both.
objectives: Solve the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) problem using 2D dynamic programming
Define a DP state over two string positions
Distinguish a subsequence from a substring

sections.text: 1. Recap: Two Indices, Now Both Into Strings
Knapsack's two indices were an item and a capacity. LCS's two indices are positions in two different strings — the two-index 2D DP pattern keeps reappearing with a new meaning each time.
A subsequence keeps characters in their original relative order but does NOT require them to be contiguous — unlike a substring, which must be an unbroken run of characters (recall Strings).
"ACE" is a subsequence of "ABCDE" (skip B and D) but is NOT a substring of it, since those characters aren't contiguous in the original string.

sections.code:
// Given two strings: "ABCDE" and "ACE"
// Find the length of their LONGEST COMMON SUBSEQUENCE

sections.text: dp[i][j] represents the length of the longest common subsequence between the first i characters of string s1 and the first j characters of string s2.
A matching character always extends a diagonal-neighbor answer by exactly one. A non-match never adds length — it just carries forward the best of dropping one character from either string.

sections.title: Why the answer is 3
points: C matches C: continuing along the diagonal, dp[3][2] = dp[2][1] + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2
The shared subsequence is literally "ACE" — dp[5][3] = 3 confirms its length

sections.title: Diffing two documents to see what content survived between edits
sections.text: A document diff tool comparing an old and new version of a file finds the longest sequence of lines common to both, in the same order, even if lines were inserted or deleted in between — that shared backbone is exactly a Longest Common Subsequence, letting the tool highlight only the lines that actually changed.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing subsequence with substring — LCS does NOT require the matching characters to be contiguous in either string.
Writing dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 unconditionally, without checking s1[i-1] == s2[j-1] first.
Forgetting the max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) fallback on a non-match — skipping this loses the best answer found so far along either string.
Off-by-one errors between the 1-indexed dp table and the 0-indexed strings — s1[i-1] and s2[j-1] are the characters dp[i][j] is currently comparing, not s1[i] and s2[j].

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does dp[i][j] represent in the LCS problem?
questions.answer: The length of the longest common subsequence between the first i characters of s1 and the first j characters of s2.

questions.question: What is the LCS recurrence?
questions.answer: If s1[i-1] == s2[j-1], dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Otherwise, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).

questions.question: What is the difference between a subsequence and a substring?
questions.answer: A subsequence preserves relative order but doesn't need to be contiguous; a substring must be an unbroken, contiguous run of characters.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Longest Common Subsequence applies the two-index 2D DP pattern to a pair of strings: dp[i][j] is the length of the longest common subsequence between the first i characters of s1 and the first j characters of s2. When the current characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1, extending a shared subsequence diagonally. When they don't match, dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]), carrying forward the best result found by dropping a character from either string. Unlike a substring, a subsequence doesn't need contiguous characters — only preserved relative order — which is what makes this DP formulation necessary rather than a simple substring scan.

sections.title: Trick to Remember LCS
sections.text: LCS: characters match → go diagonal, +1. Characters don't match → take the best of up or left. Subsequence = order kept, gaps allowed.

sections.title: Longest Common Subsequence
sections.problemStatement: Given two strings text1 and text2, return the length of their longest common subsequence, or 0 if none exists.
hints: This is the lesson's own worked example — try implementing it yourself before checking back against the full trace above.
State: dp[i][j] = LCS length between text1's first i characters and text2's first j characters.
Match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. No match: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Full 2D dp table, filled row by row.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(m * n) for the full 2D table

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Each row only depends on the row directly above — collapse to two rolling rows.
